did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a robust and interpretable deep learning framework for multi-category skin cancer categorization. Specifically, it employs an ensemble of lightweight CNN architectures (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, and DenseNet121) combined via feature fusion, trained on the HAM10000 dataset. It also integrates Grad-CAM for interpretability and was deployed on a Raspberry Pi 5.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new deep learning framework (ensemble of MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, and DenseNet121) for multi-category skin cancer categorization. It details specific methods (feature fusion, Grad-CAM, preprocessing on HAM10000 dataset), reports original results (98.25% accuracy, 0.01s inference time on Raspberry Pi 5), and includes a comparative analysis with existing models. Although published in a conference proceedings (COMPUTINGCON), it contains original technical results and validation, fitting the definition of a research paper.

title: A Robust and Interpretable Deep Learning-Based System for Multi-Category Skin Cancer Categorization on Resource-Constrained Edge Devices
journal: 2025 International Conference on Computing and Communications (COMPUTINGCON)
brief_summary: This study proposes a robust and interpretable deep learning framework for multi-category skin cancer categorization optimized for resource-constrained edge devices. The system employs an ensemble of lightweight CNN architectures—MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, and DenseNet121—combined via feature fusion and trained on the HAM10000 dataset. Advanced preprocessing, data augmentation, and class-balancing techniques were applied to enhance generalizability, while Grad-CAM was integrated to provide visual explanations of model decisions. The proposed model achieved an accuracy of 98.25% with an inference time of 0.01 seconds on a Raspberry Pi 5. These results demonstrate the feasibility of deploying real-time, portable, and reliable skin cancer screening systems in underserved healthcare settings.

Mentioned in the Conclusion as one of the 7 classes the model was trained to categorize, described as 'the most frequently overlooked one'. Note: This class is not explicitly listed in the per-class performance table (Table II) but is referenced as part of the seven-class system.

all_task_types.target: Seven predefined categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, and Tinea Corporis/Ringworm)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification task on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories by assigning a class probability to each possible skin lesion category and selecting the label with the highest probability. Evaluated using accuracy (98.25%), precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrix, and ROC curves.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: A large and highly diverse set of 10,015 dermatoscopic images created in the study of skin lesions. The collection is enriched through sources including the ViDIR Group at the Medical University of Vienna and Queensland University in Australia. Images are annotated by professional dermatologists, standardized to 600x450 pixel resolution in JPEG format, and are publicly accessible.

use.training: The dataset was used for training the proposed ensemble deep learning model (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121) after preprocessing, data augmentation, and class balancing.
use.validation: The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets, though specific counts for validation and testing splits were not reported in the text.
use.testing: The dataset was used for testing the model's performance, achieving 98.25% accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the consistency of prediction and stability of training, and to measure the accuracy of classification across seven skin lesion categories.
To assess the model's ability to differentiate between skin lesion types across various threshold settings and to calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for reliability.
To provide an in-depth picture of model outcomes beyond accuracy, particularly for imbalanced datasets, by quantifying the Average Precision (AP) for each class.
To measure the predictive character of the model and its ability to discriminate skin lesions of various types, confirming symmetric generalization and clinical utility.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices, ROC curves, and PR curves.

HAM10000.experiment: Proposed Ensemble CNN (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121) with Feature Fusion

Proposed Model.AUC: Nearly or more than 0.90 for all classes
Proposed Model.AP (AK, DF, Vascular Lesions): Lower due to fewer samples and inter-class similarity

HAM10000.notes: The model was deployed on a Raspberry Pi 5. Grad-CAM was used for interpretability. The dataset was imbalanced, with Vascular Lesions having the lowest support (16) and Melanocytic Nevi the highest (320). Misclassifications were primarily between visually similar classes like Melanoma and Nevi.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of lightweight CNN architectures: MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, and DenseNet121, combined via a feature fusion strategy.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was used; models were fine-tuned with final fully connected layers replaced and retrained on the HAM10000 dataset. The text notes these models were selected for their lightweight nature and compatibility with edge devices, implying ImageNet pretraining common to these architectures, though specific pretraining weights are not explicitly named beyond 'pre-learned'.

architecture_details.fusion_method: Feature fusion strategy combining the ensemble models
architecture_details.convolutional_layers: Multiple convolutional layers with 3x3 filters, followed by ReLU activation and Max Pooling (2x2 window)
architecture_details.dense_layers: One or more dense (fully connected) layers with linear transformation and non-linear activation
architecture_details.output_layer: Softmax classification layer for K=7 target classes

training_details.early_stopping: Implemented with patience of 10 consecutive epochs without improvement in validation loss
training_details.preprocessing: Min-max scaling, 2D Gaussian filter for noise removal, class balancing via oversampling, data augmentation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Ensemble CNN (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121 with Feature Fusion)
preprocessing_techniques: Pixel intensity normalization using min-max scaling to scale values to the range [0,1]
Optional conversion from RGB color space to grayscale or HSV color spaces
Noise removal using a 2D Gaussian filter
Data cleaning by discarding images with missing labels, corrupted pixels, or duplication
Class balancing via oversampling techniques to handle imbalanced dataset
Data augmentation to improve model generalization and robustness
Transformation of categorical labels into one-hot encoded vectors

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to improve model generalization and robustness. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) were not reported in the text, only that the technique was used.
Used to handle the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset. Synthetic samples for minority classes were generated using the formula: N_Synthetic = N_Majority - N_Minority.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble deep learning framework, combining MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, and DenseNet121 via feature fusion, achieved a classification accuracy of 98.25% and an F1-score of 0.95 on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset.

The model demonstrated high clinical utility and symmetric generalization across seven skin lesion categories, including difficult-to-detect classes like Actinic Keratosis and Dermatofibroma, with Vascular Lesions achieving perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores (1.00).
The system exhibited robust interpretability through Grad-CAM visualizations, which confirmed the model's focus on relevant dermoscopic features, thereby enhancing potential clinical trust.

The model achieved an inference time of 0.01 seconds on a Raspberry Pi 5, demonstrating its feasibility for real-time, portable, and reliable skin cancer screening on resource-constrained edge devices.

While SkinWiseNet reported higher accuracy (99.86%), the proposed model offers a reasonable trade-off between reliability and hardware costs, particularly given its optimized performance on low-power edge hardware compared to other baselines like MetaFormer and SkinMultiNet.

The study confirms the potential of lightweight, interpretable deep learning models to democratize dermatological diagnostics and improve early detection outcomes in underserved healthcare settings with limited access to specialized expertise.

Future work will focus on integrating attention mechanisms or hybrid transformer-CNN architectures to extract more features from dermoscopic images and employing domain adaptation methods to enhance generalizability across different imaging devices and populations.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which introduces potential population and dataset bias as the data is enriched from specific sources (ViDIR Group, Queensland University) and may not fully represent global skin tone diversity or imaging conditions.
The dataset is significantly imbalanced, with Vascular Lesions having only 16 samples and Melanocytic Nevi having 320, which may skew performance metrics despite the use of oversampling techniques.

The model achieved perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores (1.00) for Vascular Lesions, a class with very low support (n=16), which may indicate overfitting or lack of generalizability for this rare class.
The proposed model's accuracy (98.25%) is lower than the comparative baseline SkinWiseNet (99.86%), though the paper argues for a trade-off favoring edge deployment feasibility.
Inference time was only reported for the proposed model (0.01s) and MetaFormer (0.10s); inference times for SkinWiseNet and SkinMultiNet were not reported, limiting direct performance comparison.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying instead on descriptive metrics and visualizations (ROC/PR curves).

External validation was not performed; the model was trained and tested on the same dataset (HAM10000), raising concerns about generalizability to unseen clinical populations or different imaging devices.

The inclusion of 'Tinea Corporis' (Ringworm) as a class is notable as it is not a skin cancer, and its support count was not explicitly detailed in the per-class table, though it was part of the seven-class system.
Deployment was validated only on a Raspberry Pi 5; results may not generalize to other edge devices with different computational constraints or hardware architectures.
